critic_raw:
This is a carefully written design with several genuine strengths: temporal label gating, preprocessing fit-on-train-only, single held-out test set, operationally grounded primary metric, and explicit scope limitation. However, there are three substantive methodological issues worth raising.

---

**Issue 1: Stratified random split vs. temporal ordering — a fundamental threat to validity**

The claim: Stratified random sampling at the window level produces a valid holdout for evaluating production generalization.

Why this is wrong: Each account contributes multiple overlapping 90-day rolling windows across 36 months. A stratified random split will place windows from the same account in both train and test sets. The LSTM/transformer will learn latent account-level representations (spending patterns, KYC attributes, risk tier) that are effectively memorized during training; when a later window from the same account appears in the test set, the model benefits from having seen earlier windows from that account. The aggregated baseline is far less susceptible to this because its features are stationary summaries — there is no sequential state to leak. The result is that the sequence model's advantage on the test set is partially (possibly substantially) attributable to account-level memorization rather than the ability to detect novel suspicious patterns. In production, the model is always applied to windows from customers whose full history was not necessarily seen at training time, or to customers exhibiting newly emergent typologies. The stratified random split conflates in-distribution interpolation with out-of-distribution generalization.

Note also that the 90-day label gating prevents label leakage from future enforcement actions, but does not prevent the model from seeing transactions that temporally overlap with labeled suspicious windows in the training set. If account A has a suspicious window in months 10–12 and a 'not suspicious' window in months 9–11, those windows share transactions, and if one goes to train and the other to test, there is transaction-level overlap across the split.

What would constitute evidence: Compare the proposed stratified random split to a strict temporal split (train on months 1–24, validate on 25–30, test on 31–36). If the sequence model's advantage shrinks substantially under the temporal split, account-level leakage is the likely cause. Additionally, compute the fraction of test-set accounts that also appear in the training set — if >90% (typical in retail banking), the overlap is material.

---

**Issue 2: The baseline is not a fair isolation of 'sequence structure'**

The claim: The baseline receives aggregated merchant graph statistics, so any gap is attributable to sequence structure and graph features, not model sophistication.

Why this is partially wrong (and misleading): The proposed model receives both sequence features AND graph features (degree centrality, clustering coefficient, community membership per counterparty, embedded merchant IDs). The baseline receives only aggregate graph statistics (average merchant degree, fraction of high-degree counterparties). These are not equivalent graph representations — the baseline loses community structure and per-transaction graph context. The confound is therefore not just 'sequence structure' but also 'richer graph representation.' If the sequence model wins, the experiment cannot attribute the gain to sequential modeling vs. aggregation; it could be entirely due to superior graph feature construction.

A cleaner design would include a third condition: an aggregated model that also receives the full graph features (degree centrality, clustering coefficient, community membership) aggregated across the window. This isolates sequential modeling from graph feature richness.

What would constitute evidence: Ablate the graph features from the sequence model and compare to the baseline. If the gap disappears or reverses, the graph representation — not the LSTM/transformer — is doing the work.

---

**Issue 3: Precision@85%-recall is computed on a fixed held-out prevalence, making it a biased estimator of operational precision**

The claim: Precision at fixed recall is robust to class imbalance and directly reflects operational objectives.

Why this is incomplete: Precision at fixed recall is a function of both the model's likelihood ratio surface and the base rate in the evaluation set. Stratified sampling preserves the observed suspicious-case rate in the historical data, but in production, the true positive rate varies by: calendar period (typology evolution, regulatory focus), customer segment mix, and upstream rule-based pre-filtering that selects which accounts enter the monitoring system. If the test set's base rate is 1.2% and production's effective base rate is 0.4% (because the pre-filter changed or a new customer segment was onboarded), the operational precision will be dramatically lower than reported — even if AUROC is identical. The design acknowledges that results don't generalize to other institutions but says nothing about base-rate sensitivity within the same institution over time.

This is not a fatal flaw but it means the precision@recall figure is a relative comparison between two models, not an absolute operational estimate. The design should include a sensitivity analysis: report precision@85%-recall as a function of synthetic base rate (e.g., 0.5x, 1x, 2x the observed prevalence) to bracket the operational range.

What would constitute evidence: Plot precision@85%-recall vs. assumed prevalence for both models. If the precision advantage of the sequence model is stable across the prevalence range, the comparison is robust. If it reverses or narrows to insignificance at lower prevalences, the conclusion is fragile.

---

**Summary**

Issue 1 (data split) is the most serious: it directly threatens the causal attribution the experiment is designed to establish, and it preferentially biases the sequence model over the baseline. Issue 2 (graph feature confound) makes the source of any observed gain ambiguous even if the split were corrected. Issue 3 (precision@recall and base rate) is a reporting concern rather than a design flaw, but it weakens the operationalization claim. The design is not fatally broken, but Issue 1 alone is sufficient to cast doubt on the main conclusion.
defender_raw:
## Pass 1 — Analysis of Anticipated Criticisms

### 1. Temporal Leakage via Random Stratified Split

**What a critic would claim:** Using stratified random sampling (rather than a strict temporal split) creates data leakage. 90-day rolling windows from the same account at different points in time will share transactions and potentially share label signal. If window t=30 and window t=60 overlap by 60 days, and one is in train while the other is in test, the model sees future information implicitly.

**Is the claim valid?** Partially. The design specifies windows are sampled "at the window level" and stratified, but does not explicitly describe how overlapping windows from the same account are handled. This is a real gap. Rolling 90-day windows at typical monthly or weekly step sizes will produce heavily overlapping sequences. If account A has windows Jan-Mar and Feb-Apr, and Jan-Mar ends up in training while Feb-Apr ends up in test, 60 days of transactions are shared, and since labels are derived from case closures on the same account, label correlation is high. This is a meaningful threat to validity.

**However, the severity is partially mitigated:** The label comes from a case closure event tied to a specific window's final transaction date (with 90-day minimum observation). If windows are non-overlapping (stepped by 90 days), this problem disappears. The design does not specify step size, which is a real ambiguity. If step size equals window size (non-overlapping), the stratified split is defensible because examples are genuinely independent at the account-window level. I would concede this is an unresolved ambiguity that the design should have made explicit.

**What remains uncertain:** Whether windows are overlapping or non-overlapping. This needs to be specified.

---

### 2. Temporal Split vs. Stratified Random Split

**What a critic would claim:** A temporally ordered split (train on months 1–24, validate on 25–30, test on 31–36) is the gold standard for time-series financial data. The stratified random split means the test set contains windows from throughout the 36-month period, violating temporal realism.

**Is the claim valid?** Yes, this is a legitimate methodological concern. In AML, transaction patterns and typologies shift over time. A temporally ordered split tests whether the model generalizes forward in time, which is the actual deployment scenario. The stratified random split as described means the test set is not a genuine forward holdout — it is a random sample of the same distribution as training data. This inflates apparent generalizability.

**What partially defends the design:** The design does include a secondary diagnostic — stratifying test performance by window month to detect late-period degradation. This partially addresses the concern but does not replace a proper temporal split. The comparison between sequence and aggregated models might still be valid if both suffer equally from temporal optimism, but absolute metric values are overstated.

**Verdict on this point:** This is a real flaw. The temporal split diagnostic is insufficient to replace a proper temporal holdout for the primary split. I would concede this needs to be remedied or at least prominently caveated.

---

### 3. Label Quality and Conservative Labeling Strategy

**What a critic would claim:** Labeling sequences without confirmed case closure as 'not suspicious' is noisy. Many true suspicious activities are never investigated (systemic under-detection), and many investigated cases are closed as not suspicious due to resource constraints rather than true innocence.

**Is the claim valid?** Yes, this is a known and unavoidable limitation of AML datasets. The design acknowledges it is a conservative labeling approach. The 90-day minimum observation window is a reasonable practical choice that reduces the worst form of label leakage (labeling based on incomplete investigations). However, it does not solve the fundamental class impurity problem.

**What the design gets right:** The 90-day window is well-motivated, the design uses the bank's own case closure records (ground truth within the bank's operational reality), and the design explicitly scopes the experiment to "the bank's policies and transaction distribution." This scoping is honest and appropriate. The label quality issue affects all methods equally, so it does not differentially bias the comparison between sequence and aggregated models — it only reduces the absolute sensitivity of the evaluation.

**What remains uncertain:** Whether the recall floor of 85% is achievable given the true positive label noise. Calibration metrics (Brier score, ECE) are included, which will surface systematic miscalibration from label noise.

---

### 4. Bidirectional LSTM vs. Deployment Reality

**What a critic would claim:** A bidirectional LSTM uses future context within the window, which may not be available in real-time streaming scenarios. The experiment measures batch performance, not online detection performance.

**Is the claim valid?** This is a fair practical concern, but not a validity concern for the experiment as designed. The experiment explicitly evaluates 90-day retrospective windows — a batch scoring scenario. In many AML systems, SAR filing is inherently retrospective (cases are built over weeks). The bidirectional architecture is appropriate for this evaluation context. The design does not claim to evaluate streaming detection. I would defend this fully.

---

### 5. Fairness of Model Comparison (Feature Asymmetry)

**What a critic would claim:** The sequence model gets transaction-level sequences AND merchant graph features AND temporal statistics. The baseline only gets 90-day aggregates. This is not a fair ablation — you are varying both the model architecture and the feature set simultaneously.

**Is the claim valid?** This is the central tension of the experiment. The design explicitly states the goal is to isolate the value of "sequence structure and graph features" together. This is a reasonable research question — does the combination of richer features + sequence modeling outperform aggregated features + simple models? — but it does not isolate which component drives the gain.

**What the design gets right:** It is transparent about this. The design states it isolates "sequence structure as the source of gain" through controlled feature volume. But a critic is right that if the sequence model wins, we cannot determine whether the win comes from (a) the LSTM capturing order, (b) graph features not available to the baseline, or (c) both. The baseline does receive aggregated graph statistics (average degree, fraction of high-degree counterparties), but not full node-level graph embeddings.

**Proposed remedy that the design lacks:** A second baseline — GBT trained on all the same features as the sequence model but flattened/aggregated — would cleanly isolate sequential modeling value. The current design does not include this intermediate ablation.

**This is a real gap.** It doesn't invalidate the experiment, but it limits interpretability of what drives any observed gain.

---

### 6. Class Imbalance Handling

**What a critic would claim:** Balanced class weighting combined with precision@85% recall evaluation may interact badly. The threshold used to compute precision@recall is derived from the model's score distribution, and class weighting changes that distribution. Calibration metrics (Brier, ECE) may be misleading under reweighting.

**Is the claim valid?** This is a nuanced but legitimate concern. However, the design evaluates at a fixed operating point derived from the validation set threshold sweep, and both models receive identical treatment. The comparison is internally consistent. Calibration metrics under class reweighting should be reported with this caveat (the design notes Brier and ECE as secondary metrics, which is appropriate for flagging any pathological behavior). I would partially defend this — it is a known complexity, not an error.

---

### 7. Merchant Graph Embeddings Computed on Training Window Only

**What a critic would claim:** Fixing merchant graph embeddings from the training period means the graph does not reflect the test period's merchant network. New merchants appearing in the test period will have no embedding.

**Is the claim valid?** Yes, this is a real cold-start / graph staleness problem. The design states embeddings are "held fixed" — this is operationally realistic (you can't retrain the graph model in production for every window), but it means the test-period merchant graph coverage may degrade, particularly for newly formed merchant clusters that are precisely where money laundering typologies evolve. This is a limitation the design should acknowledge explicitly.

**Defense:** For a 36-month study where the test set is randomly sampled from the same period (not purely from the final months), merchant coverage degradation is limited. If a proper temporal split were used, this problem would be more severe.

---

### 8. Hyperparameter Tuning Budget Equality

**What a critic would claim:** "Equal tuning effort" is vague. Random search with the same number of iterations may explore the LSTM's much larger hyperparameter space less thoroughly than the GBT's smaller space, disadvantaging the sequence model or vice versa.

**Is the claim valid?** This is a real concern but common to nearly all neural-vs-classical comparisons. The design at least acknowledges equal tuning effort. The defense is that the LSTM/transformer with dropout and 64–128 hidden units is a relatively constrained architecture, and the random search space is not dramatically larger than GBT. This is a minor concern, not a fatal flaw.

---

## Summary of Critical Findings from Pass 1

1. **Temporal split design is flawed.** Stratified random split does not test temporal generalization. The per-month diagnostic is insufficient. This is a real methodological problem.
2. **Window overlap is unspecified.** If 90-day windows are stepped at less than 90-day intervals, overlapping windows from the same account may appear across train/test splits, creating leakage.
3. **Ablation gap.** The comparison confounds model architecture with feature set. A flattened-feature baseline is missing.
4. **Graph embedding staleness.** Acknowledged implicitly but not quantified.

Critical points 1 and 2 relate to data split validity, which is foundational. Point 3 limits interpretability of what drives the result. These are not trivially scoped away.

---

## Pass 2 — Verdict Selection

The experiment has clear design intent and several strong elements: the labeling strategy with 90-day observation is well-motivated, the metric choice (precision@recall) is operationally grounded, preprocessing discipline is correct, and the scope is appropriately bounded. However, Pass 1 reveals multiple substantive problems:

- The temporal split design undermines the validity of absolute metric claims and partially undermines the comparison (if both models overfit to the full 36-month distribution rather than being tested on forward generalization).
- The window overlap ambiguity is unresolved and could introduce leakage.
- The ablation design means that even a clear win for the sequence model is not cleanly interpretable.

These are not "interesting open questions" — they are structural issues that would need to be addressed before results could be trusted for publication or consequential deployment decisions. The per-month diagnostic does not substitute for proper temporal ordering in the primary split.

The appropriate verdict is **critique_wins** because my own analysis identifies multiple critical unaddressed flaws: the split strategy does not test temporal generalization (the core operational validity requirement for financial time-series models), window overlap is unspecified and potentially introduces leakage, and the ablation structure does not isolate sequential modeling value. Together these undermine confidence in the experimental conclusions.
all_issues_raised: Stratified random split across overlapping rolling windows from the same accounts creates transaction-level and account-level leakage into the test set, preferentially inflating the sequence model's measured advantage over the baseline.

The sequence model receives richer per-transaction graph features (community membership, per-counterparty centrality) while the baseline receives only window-level graph aggregates, so any observed gap cannot be attributed solely to sequential modeling vs. aggregation.

Precision at fixed recall is computed at the historical base rate in the evaluation set; operational precision at a different base rate (due to pre-filter changes or segment shifts) could differ substantially, and no sensitivity analysis is included.
all_issues_adjudicated: Stratified random split across overlapping rolling windows from the same accounts creates account-level and transaction-level leakage that preferentially inflates the sequence model's measured advantage, and the defender concedes this is a real flaw — the per-month diagnostic is insufficient to substitute for a proper temporal holdout.

The sequence model receives richer per-transaction graph features (community membership, per-counterparty centrality) while the baseline receives only window-level aggregates, so any observed performance gap cannot be attributed solely to sequential modeling; the defender concedes a flattened-feature intermediate baseline is missing.

Precision at fixed recall is evaluated at the historical base rate with no sensitivity analysis across plausible operational base rates; the defender does not substantively rebut this and the design omits the recommended prevalence sensitivity analysis.
